planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average soil in full sun; black oak grows on dry to mesic upland sites across eastern North America and is one of the most important oaks for wildlife in the red oak group. Protect newly planted acorns from squirrel predation.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; black oak is a major forest tree of the eastern United States, growing on dry to mesic upland sites from Maine to Texas and one of the primary components of the oak-hickory forest type. The leaves are large, deeply lobed, and glossy dark green, turning russet-red to orange-brown in fall. Like all oaks in the red oak group, the acorns require two years to mature and are an important high-fat food source for white-tailed deer, wild turkeys, blue jays, and many woodpeckers. The inner bark contains a yellow dye called quercitron that was historically used in textile dyeing and tanning. Black oak supports over 500 species of caterpillars and other insects in the eastern US, making it one of the most ecologically important trees for supporting bird populations. A long-lived, majestic shade tree for large properties.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; scarlet oak requires excellent drainage and performs poorly in wet, compacted, or alkaline conditions. It is particularly well-suited to dry, sandy, or rocky upland sites.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average soil in full sun; scarlet oak is named for the spectacular, deep scarlet fall color — one of the most brilliantly red of all native trees, maintaining its color for 4–6 weeks in fall. The leaves are deeply cut with pointed lobes typical of the red oak group, and the deeply cleft sinuses extend nearly to the midrib, creating a dramatic, finely cut pattern. The tree is naturally adapted to dry, infertile, sandy or rocky upland sites where other oaks struggle, and is a pioneer species on disturbed dry slopes in the East. The acorns are a critical food source for squirrels, blue jays, wood ducks, and wild turkeys. Well-suited as a street tree and park tree in well-drained, slightly acidic urban soils. The spectacular fall color and attractive form make it one of the finest native oaks for landscape use.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in very well-drained, acidic, rocky, thin soil in full sun; Georgia oak is a rare endemic of granitic flatrocks and exposed rocky outcrops of the southern Appalachians and Piedmont, specializing in conditions with thin, excessively well-drained, acidic soil over granite.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in very well-drained, acidic, rocky, thin soil in full sun; Georgia oak is a rare, small-statured oak endemic to granitic flatrock outcrops and thin, rocky soils of the southern Appalachians and Georgia-Carolina Piedmont — one of the more restricted native oaks in the eastern United States, found primarily on Stone Mountain in Georgia, Hicks Knob in Georgia, and a few scattered Piedmont locations. It is a small tree or large shrub reaching 25–30 feet, with small, deeply lobed leaves that are smaller than most red oaks, turning a reliable, rich red-orange in fall. The compact size and superb fall color make it an outstanding specimen tree for rock gardens, rocky slopes, and smaller properties in zones 6–9 where space constraints prohibit larger oaks. A significant conservation plant with a very limited natural range.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; longstalked holly is unusual among hollies in having non-spiny, smooth-margined leaves resembling those of a camellia. Plant both male and female plants for berry production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; longstalked holly is a Japanese evergreen holly quite unlike the typical image of holly — the leaves are entire, smooth-margined, and spineless, with a glossy, dark green appearance resembling camellia leaves. The small, bright red berries are borne on unusually long, slender stalks (reflecting the name 'pedunculosa' — long-stalked) that hold the berries away from the foliage in a distinctive dangling display quite unlike any other holly. One of the hardiest broad-leaved evergreen hollies, tolerating temperatures to –20°F in zone 5 with protection. Excellent bird food: the bright berries attract thrushes, waxwings, and mockingbirds in fall and winter. A refined and underused evergreen screening shrub or small tree that adds elegant, season-long interest. Requires both male and female plants for berry set.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sugar bush is an evergreen California native of chaparral and dry, rocky slopes and is fully drought-tolerant once established in appropriate dry, well-drained conditions. Summer irrigation is harmful — water only to establish.

directions: Plant in dry, well-drained, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sugar bush is an evergreen sumac native to chaparral and dry, rocky slopes of the California Coast Ranges and Baja California, one of the most reliable and ornamental drought-tolerant shrubs for California-climate gardens. The large, bold, leathery, ovate leaves are folded lengthwise like a taco shell — a distinctive feature that sheds rain and reduces water loss. The small, red, waxy-coated berries produced in summer are coated with a tart, malic-acid-rich white sugar that was mixed with water by indigenous peoples to make a refreshing, lemonade-like drink — hence the common name 'sugar bush.' A cornerstone shrub for California native and Mediterranean-climate gardens, providing year-round evergreen screening, early spring flowers for native bees, summer berries for birds, and outstanding drought tolerance. Never irrigate in summer once established — it prefers natural summer drought.
